blocks.text:
Single hanzi that are the name of a particular Chinese river. Most carry the “three-drops-of-water” radical 氵; click any character to see its readings and the river it names.
Use the 繁 / 简 / 日 switch by the set dropdown to toggle script — the two headline words and the major rivers carry their Traditional/Simplified forms; the rest are shown simplified.

blocks.title: 1 · 河 & 江 — the two words for “river”

blocks.text: Both began as proper nouns. 河 hé was the name of the Yellow River (黄河) and 江 jiāng the name of the Yangtze (长江); only later did each generalise into an ordinary word for “river.” By rough convention 河 still dominates northern river names and 江 southern ones. With 淮 (Huai) and 济 (Ji) they made up the 四渎, the Four Great Rivers of classical geography.

Each of these single characters is the familiar name of a well-known river. Several double as the one-character abbreviation for an entire province — 湘 for Hunan, 赣 for Jiangxi, 闽 for Fujian. Note the two Min Rivers: 岷 (Sichuan) and 闽 (Fujian).

blocks.cells: 淮(漢TJ汉S)湘(贛TJ赣S)(閩TJ闽S)岷浙漓渭(涇TJ泾S)洛汾灞汨(濟TJ济S)

blocks.text: Smaller but real, identifiable rivers — mostly tributaries scattered across the provinces (the Yi and Si of Shandong, the Yuan and Li of Hunan, the Tuo and Fu of Sichuan, and so on). Shown simplified.

blocks.cells: 沂泗汶淄潍洙沭漯漳淇洹溱汝汴颍涡淠濉滁溧沅澧沩涟浏沱涪滦滏洨湟洮涑沁浔浈溠滹沔

blocks.text: The long tail: characters whose entire dictionary gloss is essentially “name of a river,” a great many marked “in ancient times.” Drawn straight from Unihan and CC-CEDICT. Several are rare CJK-extension characters that may not render in every font.
